one_line_takeaway: STING shows that decomposing harmful intents into benign-seeming multi-turn phases against tool-using LLM agents roughly doubles illicit task completion versus single-turn prompting for several frontier models, while -- contrary to chatbot-era findings -- attacking in lower-resource languages does not reliably increase jailbreak success.
key_finding: STING substantially outperforms both baselines on English AgentHarm scenarios (Table 1, AHS, Smax=10 vs single-turn): Qwen3-Next 72.7±4.1 vs 35.1±6.1 (+107.1%), Claude Sonnet 4.5 32.3±5.6 vs 16.0±4.8 (+101.9%), DeepSeek-V3.2 61.8±4.7 vs 31.2±5.5 (+98.1%), GPT-5.1 34.1±5.5 vs 24.3±5.3 (+40.3%), Gemini 3 Flash 50.9±5.5 vs 45.9±6.2 (+10.8%). X-Teaming (adapted chatbot multi-turn baseline) performed worse than even single-turn prompting in this agentic setting (e.g., Qwen3-Next 27.0±4.9). Reducing the strategy budget from Smax=10 to Smax=5 degraded STING's AHS by only ~9.4% on average, still beating both baselines. Across languages, unlike prior chatbot-focused multilingual jailbreak findings, lower-resource languages (Urdu, Telugu) did not consistently increase jailbreak success; hazard ratios (Table 2) were mixed and model-dependent (e.g., for Qwen3-Next, Hindi and Telugu had hazard ratios below 1 -- i.e., lower susceptibility than English -- attributed largely to imperfect multilingual tool use inflating AHS without satisfying the intent-completion grader). AHS and ASR were highly correlated (Pearson's r=0.96) but treated as complementary. Benign agentic capability (Table 3) was similar across languages, ruling out degraded general capability as an explanation. Reasoning-effort ablations showed a non-monotonic safety effect: Qwen3-Next's Thinking variant was consistently safer than Instruct (-14.5% AHS English, -14.4% Ukrainian, -12.5% Telugu), and for GPT-5.1 medium reasoning was safer than both none and high reasoning (-4.3% AHS vs high, on average). Tool-name hints increased AHS by 8.5% on average (Table 4) but had mixed effects on ASR. Among defenses (Table 5, Qwen3-Next, Smax=5), the Safety Prompt gave the largest harmful-task AHS reduction (-38.9% English, -33.3% to -40.5% across languages) at a modest benign-task cost (-5.7% to -5.9%), Omni-Moderation gave moderate reductions (-2.4% to -17.2%), and PromptGuard-86M had limited effect (-3.8% to 0% ) despite very low false-flag rate on benign prompts.
core_contribution: The paper introduces STING (Sequential Testing of Illicit N-step Goal execution), an automated multi-agent red-teaming framework for tool-using LLM agents. Given a harmful intent, a Strategist agent synthesizes a benign-appearing persona and decomposes the intent into a sequence of atomic phases; an Attacker agent embodies the persona and pursues each phase turn-by-turn against a target agent; a Refusal Detector and a Phase-Completion Checker judge each target response and drive the Attacker to retry or advance. The authors also build a language-agnostic multilingual extension (translated AgentHarm tools/rubrics across 7 languages) and a survival-analysis framework that models multi-turn red-teaming as a time-to-first-jailbreak random variable, from which they derive Kaplan-Meier discovery curves, Cox proportional-hazards ratios attributing risk to covariates like attack language, and a new summary metric, Restricted Mean Jailbreak Discovery (RMJD).
threat_model: An adversary interacts with a tool-using LLM agent (not a plain chatbot) over multiple conversational turns under a benign-looking persona, incrementally steering the agent through a decomposed sequence of sub-tasks that individually appear innocuous but jointly realize a harmful/illegal end goal (e.g., generating and posting a fabricated 'authentic' video of a politician). The adversary is budget-constrained: at most Smax attack strategies and Tmax turns per strategy (set to 10 and 10 in experiments). The paper studies this across both English and 6 non-English languages (Chinese, French, Ukrainian, Hindi, Urdu, Telugu), and also considers an adversary with/without knowledge of the target's tool names.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) it focuses on measurement/analysis rather than novel attacks or deployable defenses, explicitly limiting operational detail to mitigate dual-use risk; (2) the multilingual analysis relies on machine-translated tools/prompts and rubrics, and the authors observe that imperfect multilingual tool usage can conflate AHS and true intent completion, motivating their recommendation to treat AHS and ASR as complementary rather than interchangeable; (3) defenses tested are lightweight/off-the-shelf (a prompt classifier, a moderation API, and a single safety system prompt) rather than adaptive or STING-aware defenses; (4) target-agent coverage varies by language (multilingual results limited to 3 of the 5 target models; Claude Sonnet 4.5 and DeepSeek-V3.2 evaluated English-only); (5) the Strategist model (Gemini 3 Pro) generates strategies in English for all languages by default, though the authors note an appendix check with target-language strategy generation; (6) the work explicitly flags dual-use risk of releasing a systematized multi-turn attack-decomposition framework.

datasets_used: AgentHarm public test set (44 base behaviors x 4 prompt variants = 176 prompt instances), including its benign subset used for false-refusal/benign-capability checks
quantitative_results: AHS Smax=10 vs single-turn: Qwen3-Next 72.7±4.1 vs 35.1±6.1 (+107.1%)
AHS Smax=10 vs single-turn: Claude Sonnet 4.5 32.3±5.6 vs 16.0±4.8 (+101.9%)
AHS Smax=10 vs single-turn: DeepSeek-V3.2 61.8±4.7 vs 31.2±5.5 (+98.1%)
AHS Smax=10 vs single-turn: GPT-5.1 34.1±5.5 vs 24.3±5.3 (+40.3%)
AHS Smax=10 vs single-turn: Gemini 3 Flash 50.9±5.5 vs 45.9±6.2 (+10.8%)
STING Smax=5 vs Smax=10: -9.4% average AHS degradation
Human validation: refusal judge precision/recall 0.98/0.93 (570 samples); intent judge 0.99/0.94 (260 samples)
Reasoning ablation: Qwen3-Next Thinking vs Instruct AHS reduction -14.5% (English), -14.4% (Ukrainian), -12.5% (Telugu)
Reasoning ablation: GPT-5.1 medium vs high reasoning -4.3% AHS on average
Tool-name-hint effect: +8.5% AHS on average across models (Table 4)
Defense ablation (Qwen3-Next, Smax=5, Table 5): Safety Prompt harmful-task AHS reduction -38.9% (English) to -33.3%/-40.5% across non-English languages, benign-task cost -5.7% to -5.9%
Defense ablation: Omni-Moderation harmful-task reduction -2.4% to -17.2%, benign cost 0% to -1.1%
Defense ablation: PromptGuard-86M harmful-task reduction -3.8% to 0%, only 2 false-flagged benign English prompts
baselines_compared: Single-turn AgentHarm prompting (original and machine-translated via GPT-5.1)
X-Teaming (Rahman et al. 2025) -- chatbot-oriented multi-turn jailbreak framework adapted to the same tool environment, Smax=5
